description: Semantic type constraints for relation families used by the active CrossExtend-KG runtime.

propagation.description: Causal or fault propagation edges where the head can lead to the tail.
examples: thermal runaway causes fire -> Fault -> Fault
voltage drop triggers alarm -> Signal -> Fault
high temperature accelerates degradation -> State -> Fault
warped flange causes boundary leak -> Component -> Fault
semantic_rules: The head must be able to act as a causal source.
The tail must be a resulting fault, signal, or state.

structural.description: Physical containment or part-of hierarchy edges.
examples: battery pack contains cells -> Asset -> Component
sensor belongs to controller -> Component -> Component
vehicle has battery pack -> Asset -> Asset
semantic_rules: Only physical entities participate in structural hierarchy.
Asset is a top-level industrial asset and Component is a subassembly or part.
Signal and State are not structural entities.

task_dependency.description: Execution or task-output dependencies where the head performs, triggers, measures, or confirms the tail.
examples: inspection observes coolant odor -> Task -> Signal
inspection verifies dry condition -> Task -> State
semantic_rules: The head must be able to execute or produce something.
The tail may be a next task, an observed output, or a discovered condition.
Component and Asset may appear as tails but not as executing heads.

communication.description: Diagnostic evidence or information-flow edges where an observable item indicates another concept.
examples: fresh wetting indicates connector leakage -> Signal -> Fault
wet after shutdown indicates leak persistence -> State -> Fault
semantic_rules: The head must be able to produce, carry, or embody diagnostic evidence.
Signal and State heads are allowed when an observation or standing condition indicates another concept.
Workflow steps and document artifacts are not valid communication heads.

lifecycle.description: State transition or experience edges where the head changes into or undergoes the tail.
examples: normal transitions to warning -> State -> State
connector changes from wet to dry -> Component -> State
fault persists as dry condition fails -> Fault -> State
semantic_rules: The head must be able to undergo a state or lifecycle change.
The tail must be a resulting state.
State -> State captures explicit state transitions.
Component/Fault -> State captures a diagnosed or verified change in condition.

_metadata.purpose: Provide semantic type validation for relation families during graph assembly.
_metadata.usage: Any triple that violates these family constraints is exported as rejected_type.
